one_line_takeaway: A broad, well-organized taxonomic survey of smart grid security that maps attacks and defenses across a proposed physical/cyber-physical/cyber three-layer model and highlights coordinated attacks, adversarial ML against ML defenses, and LLMs as key future concerns -- but contributes taxonomy and synthesis, not original experiments.
key_finding: The central thesis is that conventional defenses (cryptography, authentication, IDS) are individually insufficient for the heterogeneous, cross-layer smart grid, and that no single emerging technique is a silver bullet: game theory assumes rational adversaries and scales poorly; graph/GNN, blockchain, and ML methods each carry their own limitations; and crucially, ML-based defenses are themselves vulnerable to adversarial ML (evasion/poisoning), so deploying them shifts rather than closes the attack surface. Coordinated multi-layer attacks are called out as especially severe and hard to detect. The authors argue a holistic three-layer view is needed because vulnerabilities at one layer propagate to others, and they position LLMs as a promising but double-edged future tool (useful for interpreting attack context and zero-day patterns, yet susceptible to targeted manipulation and requiring secure-deployment safeguards).
core_contribution: A comprehensive literature survey (SoK-style) of smart grid cybersecurity by Zibaeirad et al. (UNC Charlotte / University of North Texas). The paper's OWN contributions, distinct from the works it cites, are: (1) a generalized three-layer reference framework for the smart grid (physical, cyber-physical, cyber), each with sub-layers, proposed as a unification of prior 4-layer [29] and 6-layer [30] models; (2) a structured taxonomy of attacks across those layers, organized into cyber-layer attacks (Table II), cyber-physical-layer attacks (Table III), and coordinated/hybrid attacks that span layers (Table IV); (3) a critical comparative review of four families of emerging detection/mitigation techniques -- game theory (Table V), graph theory (Table VI), blockchain (Table VII), and machine learning split into five paradigms: supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, ensemble, reinforcement (Table VIII) -- each accompanied by a 'research challenges' discussion; and (4) a forward-looking treatment of two topics the authors argue are underexplored in prior smart-grid surveys: the potential of large language models (LLMs) for zero-day/attack-pattern detection and the emerging threat of adversarial machine learning against the very ML defenses being deployed. Table I positions this survey against five prior surveys on ~14 criteria.
threat_model: As a survey the paper catalogs an aggregate threat landscape rather than a single threat model. Adversaries range from opportunistic actors to nation-state APTs, grounded in real incidents the paper cites (Stuxnet 2010, Ukraine grid attack 2015, Dragonfly 2.0 2017, BlackEnergy). Goals are framed against the CIA triad -- disrupting power supply (availability), manipulating measurements/control (integrity), and exposing sensitive/consumer data (confidentiality) -- plus physical damage to grid components. Concrete attack vectors it taxonomizes include man-in-the-middle, DoS/DDoS and botnet-driven DDoS, false data injection (FDI, including 'blind' FDI without full system knowledge), GPS spoofing / time-synchronization attacks, and coordinated attacks (topology, data-injection, load-changing, replay, and DoS variants) that chain cyber and cyber-physical actions to mask or amplify impact. A distinctive second-order threat the paper emphasizes is adversarial machine learning -- data-poisoning and (more prevalent) evasion attacks using adversarial examples -- that targets the ML-based detectors themselves, plus prompt/robustness risks specific to LLM-based defenses.
limitations: Being a survey, it offers no empirical validation, no reproducible artifacts, and no independent benchmarking of the techniques it compares; effectiveness claims are inherited from cited papers and not verified. The comparison in Table I uses self-selected criteria and coarse qualitative marks (fully/partial/none), which favors the authors' own coverage. The LLM and adversarial-ML sections are largely prospective/speculative rather than evidence-driven. The three-layer framework is a conceptual reorganization rather than a validated model. For a knowledge graph, care is needed not to attribute surveyed results (e.g., specific accuracy numbers, named systems like Donut, SS-Deep-ID, GBSS) to this paper -- they belong to the cited primary sources.

quantitative_results: Table I: comparative positioning of this survey against 5 prior surveys across ~14 qualitative criteria (e.g., holistic cyber/cyber-physical/physical integration, game-theory coverage, graph-theory coverage, blockchain, ML, adversarial ML, LLMs), scored as fully / partially / not-addressed; the authors claim game-theory, adversarial-ML, and LLM coverage are addressed only by their own survey.
Machine-learning defenses organized into 5 paradigms in Table VIII: supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, ensemble, reinforcement learning (each with methods and limitations).

CITED (not the survey's own measurement): semi-supervised SVM reported at up to ~90% accuracy for intrusion/attack detection in refs [276],[243] -- quoted from those primary works, not benchmarked by this paper.
baselines_compared: Prior smart-grid security surveys used as comparison baselines in Table I: [3] (2023), [4] (2023), [5] (2022), [6] (2021), [7] (2020). Note: these are survey baselines for scope comparison, not experimental baselines.
